context:
Provide the name of the data management support staff consulted during the preparation of this plan and the date of consultation. Please also mention if you consulted any other support staff.

Example text:
- [Name, Surname of the Data Steward], Data Steward/Privacy team/Copyright team/ICT at the Faculty of [Faculty Name], has reviewed this DMP and ethical approval process at TU Delft on [date of review]. Some funders (such as NWO and ZonMw) require that you consult data support staff (in the case of TU Delft, the Faculty Data Steward) in the creation of your Data Management Plan.

Useful contacts:
- Data Stewards: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/
- Human Research Ethics Committee (HREC): https://www.tudelft.nl/en/about-tu-delft/strategy/integrity-policy/human-research-ethics
- Copyright Information Point: https://www.tudelft.nl/library/copyright
query: Who should I consult at TU Delft when preparing my Data Management Plan, and how do I record this consultation in the DMP?
answer:
At TU Delft you are expected to involve the appropriate research data support staff when drafting or updating a Data Management Plan (DMP), especially for funded projects (e.g. NWO, ZonMw).

**Who to consult**
- Your first point of contact is your **Faculty Data Steward**, who can advise on storage, documentation, sharing, confidentiality, and compliance with TU Delft policies.
- Depending on the project, you may also consult:
  - The **Privacy Team** (for GDPR and personal data).
  - The **Copyright Information Point** (for licences and reuse of third-party data/software): https://www.tudelft.nl/library/copyright
  - ICT or security specialists (for storage, encryption, and access control).

Data Steward contacts: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/

**How to document this in your DMP**
Use a clear sentence such as:

> [Name, Surname of the Data Steward], Data Steward at the Faculty of [Faculty Name], has reviewed this DMP and the ethical approval process at TU Delft on [date of review]. I also consulted [Privacy team / Copyright team / ICT] regarding [topic].

This makes the consultation transparent for funders and for TU Delft. Once I know your faculty/role, I can help you adapt this sentence to your specific situation.

context:
Is TU Delft the lead institution for this project?

Example answers:
- Yes, the only institution involved. This is an internally defined and funded project and is managed by TU Delft with no external institutional collaborations.
- Yes, leading the collaboration. In this project, TU Delft is leading the research design and developing the research hardware. [Partner 2] is sharing commercial data on the performance of current tools.
- No. [Partner 1] is leading the consortium. TU Delft [Partner 2], along with [Partners 4, 5, and 6], are members of the consortium. TU Delft is responsible for work packages WP1 and WP2.3.
query: How should I describe whether TU Delft is the lead institution in my Data Management Plan?
answer:
In your DMP you should clearly state TU Delft’s role in the project, because this affects responsibilities for data management, agreements, and long-term archiving.

You can choose between three typical formulations:

1. **TU Delft is the only institution**
> Yes, the only institution involved. This is an internally defined and funded project and is managed by TU Delft with no external institutional collaborations.

2. **TU Delft is the lead of a collaboration**
> Yes, leading the collaboration. In this project, TU Delft is leading the research design and developing the research hardware. [Partner 2] is sharing commercial data on the performance of current tools.

3. **TU Delft is a partner, not the lead**
> No. [Partner 1] is leading the consortium. TU Delft, along with [Partners X, Y, and Z], is a member of the consortium and is responsible for work packages [WP1, WP2, …].

For collaborations, make sure the consortium or collaboration agreement describes responsibilities for data storage, sharing, archiving and ownership. Tell me your role (e.g. PI, PhD, MSc) and I can help you phrase a version that matches your project.

context:
Provide a general description of the types of data/code you will be working with, including any re-used data/code.

Examples:
- General common data types: geospatial data (public), measurements of material parameters, microscopy images, quantitative interview/survey data, data visualisation scripts, analysis scripts (.py, .c, .m), simulation model code, physical samples (e.g. rocks, human tissue).
- Personally Identifiable Information (administrative): names and/or emails of participants, informed consent forms, key document linking identifiers to participants.
- Personally Identifiable Research Data (research): IP addresses, online usernames, demographic information (gender, date of birth, age).
query: How detailed should I be when describing the types of data and code in my DMP, including reused data and code?
answer:
In your TU Delft DMP, you should provide a concise but concrete overview of all data and code you expect to generate or reuse, so that others can understand what needs to be stored, documented, protected and shared.

Consider structuring your description into three parts:

1. **Research data you generate**
   - Describe main types, formats and sources, e.g.:
     - Geospatial datasets (e.g. NetCDF, GeoTIFF), measurements of material parameters, microscopy images.
     - Quantitative survey or interview data (e.g. CSV, SPSS, Excel).
     - Physical samples (e.g. rocks, human tissue) if relevant.

2. **Personal and administrative data**
   - Distinguish between:
     - Administrative personal data (e.g. names and email addresses used for recruitment, consent, remuneration).
     - Personal research data (e.g. IP addresses, usernames, demographic variables like age, gender, location).
   - This helps later when you describe ethical review, privacy protection and access control.

3. **Code and software**
   - Mention analysis scripts, simulation model code, and data-visualisation scripts (e.g. Python, MATLAB, C/C++), and any important tools or frameworks.
   - Indicate if you will reuse existing open-source code or proprietary software.

If you tell me your discipline and typical workflow (e.g. surveys, simulations, lab measurements), I can suggest a short, discipline-specific paragraph you can paste into your DMP.

context:
How will the data/code be collected/generated?

Examples:
- Methodologies: online surveys, field observations, output of machine learning algorithms.
- Tools/applications: MS Forms, QGIS, Python scripts.
- Re-use of data/code: via (1) an open-source licence, (2) terms of use, or (3) an agreement with the data owner.

If you have questions or concerns about copyright and/or licences of re-used materials (such as multimedia, data, and software), consult the Copyright Information Point.
- Copyright Information Point: https://www.tudelft.nl/library/copyright
- Software Finder: https://softwarefinder.tudelft.nl/
query: How should I describe how my research data and code will be collected or generated in my DMP, including any reuse of existing data or software?
answer:
In TU Delft DMPs, this section explains **how** data and code come into existence, which is important for assessing quality, reproducibility, ethics and licensing.

You can structure it as follows:

1. **Data collection and generation methods**
   - Briefly describe your main methods, e.g.:
     - Online surveys (e.g. MS Forms), lab experiments, field observations, simulations, or outputs of machine-learning models.
     - GIS-based workflows (e.g. QGIS) or sensor data collection.

2. **Software and tools used**
   - Mention key tools such as **MS Forms, QGIS, Python scripts, MATLAB**, or domain-specific software.
   - Note if you use TU Delft-approved or centrally supported tools; you can check the Software Finder for options: https://softwarefinder.tudelft.nl/

3. **Reuse of existing data or code**
   - State whether you reuse:
     - Open-source datasets/software under a clear licence.
     - Data under specific terms of use.
     - Data/code obtained via a data-sharing or collaboration agreement.
   - Always check the licence or terms of use and follow them carefully.
   - For questions on copyright and licences (e.g. datasets, images, software), contact the **Copyright Information Point**: https://www.tudelft.nl/library/copyright

If you share your faculty and methods (e.g. ML on existing datasets vs. new experimental data), I can draft a ready-to-paste paragraph for your DMP.

context:
What is the Storage location for your research data?

Guidance:
- Research data should be stored on a dedicated **Project Data Storage** drive (U:), requested via the ICT department (TopDesk). See the storage request page.
- Data on Project Data Storage is accessible only to authorised individuals (ICT provides access control).
- Confidential data should be encrypted (e.g. file or folder level using VeraCrypt).
- Password managers such as **Bitwarden** or **Dashlane** are recommended to avoid access issues.
- TU Delft ICT offers manuals for full disk encryption of TU Delft computers (BitLocker for Windows, FileVault for Mac). Support: security@tudelft.nl.

Links:
- Project Data Storage request: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- Confidential data guidance: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/research-data-management/r/manage/confidential-data/other-types-of-confidential-data
- Bitwarden: https://bitwarden.com/
- Dashlane: https://www.dashlane.com/
- Disk encryption manuals: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/it-manuals/working-remotely/encryption
- Security support: mailto:security@tudelft.nl
query: What storage location should I specify for my research data in the DMP at TU Delft, and how should I handle confidential data?
answer:
TU Delft expects research data to be stored on managed, secure infrastructure with proper access control and backup.

**Primary storage: Project Data Storage (U:)**
- The recommended location is a dedicated **Project Data Storage (U: drive)** for your project.
- It can be requested via TopDesk: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- Access is restricted to authorised project members; ICT manages permissions.

**Handling confidential and personal data**
- Follow the \"need-to-have\" principle: only people who need access should have it.
- Encrypt highly confidential data at file or folder level (e.g. VeraCrypt) and store passwords securely in a password manager such as **Bitwarden** (https://bitwarden.com/) or **Dashlane** (https://www.dashlane.com/).
- For guidance on confidential data, see: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/research-data-management/r/manage/confidential-data/other-types-of-confidential-data

**Local devices and full-disk encryption**
- If you temporarily store project data on TU Delft laptops/desktops, use full-disk encryption (BitLocker/FileVault) as described in ICT manuals:
  - https://www.tudelft.nl/en/it-manuals/working-remotely/encryption
- For security questions, you can contact **security@tudelft.nl**.

In your DMP, you can say that primary storage is the Project Data Storage (U: drive), that access is restricted to authorised project members, and that confidential data will be encrypted according to TU Delft guidance. Once I know your faculty and data sensitivity, I can help you refine the wording.

context:
Who will have access to the data/code?

Guidance:
- For personal data or other confidential data, access should be limited as much as possible, based on the \"need to have\" principle.
- Using third-party service providers (cloud storage, survey platforms, etc.) may give those providers access too and must be carefully considered, especially for personal or confidential data.
- If parties outside your research team (including third-party service providers) will have access to personal data, you must explain whether appropriate data processing agreements are in place.
- If you do not have these agreements yet, contact the Privacy Team: privacy-tud@tudelft.nl.

Useful link:
- Data processing agreements (Verwerkersovereenkomst): https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Verwerkersovereenkomst.aspx
- Privacy team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
query: How should I specify who will have access to my data and code in the DMP, especially when personal data and third-party services are involved?
answer:
In this part of the DMP, TU Delft expects you to show that access to data and code is controlled and proportionate to the sensitivity of the information.

**Within the research team**
- Clearly state that access is limited to project members who need the data to perform their tasks (\"need-to-have\" principle).
- Mention that access rights on Project Data Storage (U:) or other TU Delft systems are managed by ICT and/or the project owner.

**Third-party service providers and external collaborators**
- If you use cloud services (e.g. external survey platforms or storage providers), explain who at those providers could potentially access the data and why the platform is acceptable for your data type.
- If external collaborators (e.g. partner universities or companies) will access personal data, note that **data processing agreements** or similar contracts are needed.

Data processing agreements guidance (internal SharePoint):
- https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Verwerkersovereenkomst.aspx

**Privacy Team support**
- If you have not yet arranged the necessary agreements or are unsure if they are needed, contact the **Privacy Team** for advice and mention this in the DMP:
  - Email: privacy-tud@tudelft.nl

Describe who has access (roles, not individual names), through which systems, and under what conditions (e.g. only anonymised data shared externally). Tell me your faculty and collaboration type, and I can help you write a project-specific paragraph.

context:
Does your research involve human subjects or third-party datasets collected from human participants?

Guidance:
- If your research involves human subjects or third-party datasets collected from human participants, you are required to submit an application to the Human Research Ethics Committee (HREC).
- For guidance on the HREC application procedure, see the HREC website.
- The Guide to the Extended Personal Research Data Workflow (PRDW+) describes steps for safely working with different kinds of research data from human beings.
- If you already have approval: \"I have applied for (and received) ethical approval from the Human Research Ethics Committee on [date] with HREC application number [#12345].\"

Links:
- HREC main page: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/about-tu-delft/strategy/integrity-policy/human-research-ethics
- HREC application info: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/about-tu-delft/strategy/integrity-policy/human-research-ethics/hrec-approval-1-application
- PRDW+ guide: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/manage/confidential-data/personal-data/a-guide-to-the-extended-personal-research-data-workflow
query: When does my research require HREC review at TU Delft, and how should I refer to HREC approval in the DMP?
answer:
You must consider HREC review whenever your project involves research data from human beings.

**When HREC review is required**
- If you collect data directly from human participants (e.g. interviews, surveys, experiments, focus groups), or
- If you use third-party datasets originating from human participants that include personal or sensitive information,
then you are expected to submit an application to the **Human Research Ethics Committee (HREC)**.

More information:
- HREC: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/about-tu-delft/strategy/integrity-policy/human-research-ethics
- Application procedure: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/about-tu-delft/strategy/integrity-policy/human-research-ethics/hrec-approval-1-application
- Extended Personal Research Data Workflow (PRDW+): https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/manage/confidential-data/personal-data/a-guide-to-the-extended-personal-research-data-workflow

**How to mention HREC in your DMP**
- If you **plan** to apply:
> This project involves human participants/third-party datasets from human participants. I will submit an application to the TU Delft Human Research Ethics Committee (HREC) before starting data collection, following the guidance on the HREC website.

- If you **already have approval**:
> I have applied for and received ethical approval from the Human Research Ethics Committee on [date] with HREC application number [#12345].

Once I know what kind of human-related data you collect and from which faculty, I can help refine the description.

context:
Will you work with personal data?

Definition:
- Personal data = all information about an identified or identifiable natural person. A person is identifiable if they can be identified directly or indirectly via items such as name, address, geographic location, date of birth, IP address, or factors related to physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity.
- In general, any data relating to a living person that can uniquely identify or distinguish them counts as personal data.

Links:
- Privacy definitions (personal data): https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Privacy-definitions.aspx#personal-data
- Confidential data guidance: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/research-data-management/r/manage/confidential-data/other-types-of-confidential-data
query: How can I tell whether my project works with personal data according to TU Delft and GDPR, and how should I reflect this in the DMP?
answer:
TU Delft follows the GDPR definition of **personal data**. You are working with personal data if your dataset includes information about a living person who can be identified directly or indirectly.

**Examples of personal data**
- Direct identifiers: name, email address, student or employee number, phone number.
- Indirect identifiers: date of birth, detailed location, IP address, usernames, combinations of variables that single out an individual.
- Data related to someone’s physical, mental, economic, cultural or social identity.

TU Delft guidance (internal SharePoint):
- Personal data definition and related terms: https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Privacy-definitions.aspx#personal-data
- Other types of confidential data: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/research-data-management/r/manage/confidential-data/other-types-of-confidential-data

**How to reflect this in your DMP**
- Clearly state **whether** personal data will be processed and, if so, what types (e.g. contact details, demographics, IP addresses).
- If you process personal data, other DMP sections on ethics, legal basis, storage, access control and retention become especially important.

If you describe the variables you plan to collect, I can help you decide whether they count as personal data and suggest wording for the DMP.

context:
How will ownership of the data and intellectual property rights to the data be managed?

In case of collaboration with external parties:
- IPR framed by a **consortium agreement** or **collaboration agreement** between TU Delft and named partners.
- Possibly a **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)** for company data.
- For MSc students, IPR may be framed in a **graduation agreement** between TU Delft, the student, and the company.

In case of internal projects (no other parties):
- Internal MSc thesis projects and internal research follow TU Delft IPR and research data/software policies.

Additional guidance:
- Access conditions for multi-partner projects and multiple data owners should be outlined in the relevant agreement.
- Explain access conditions during and after the project; consider licences for data access and reuse.
- Indicate whether IPR are affected and how they are handled.
- For third-party data, check restrictions on reuse and relevant licences.

Links:
- TU Delft Intellectual Property: https://intranet.tudelft.nl/en/-/intellectual-property
- Faculty Contract Management: https://intranet.tudelft.nl/en/-/faculty-contract-management?inheritRedirect=true
- Data/software licensing: https://data.4tu.nl/info/en/use/publish-cite/upload-your-data-in-our-data-repository/licencing
- The Turing Way (licensing): https://the-turing-way.netlify.app/reproducible-research/licensing
- Copyright Information Point: https://www.tudelft.nl/library/copyright
- Data Stewardship contact: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/
- IP of students: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/student/my-study-me/rules-guidelines-and-participation/intellectual-property
query: How should I describe ownership and intellectual property rights (IPR) for my research data and code in the DMP at TU Delft, especially when external partners or students are involved?
answer:
In your DMP you should show that ownership and IPR are clarified and consistent with TU Delft policies and any collaboration agreements.

**Internal TU Delft projects (no external partners)**
- For research by TU Delft staff, data and software usually fall under TU Delft IPR, following institutional policies.
- MSc students on internal projects should agree with their supervisor how data and IPR are handled; see IP of students:
  - https://www.tudelft.nl/en/student/my-study-me/rules-guidelines-and-participation/intellectual-property

General IP info:
- https://intranet.tudelft.nl/en/-/intellectual-property

**Projects with external partners**
- When collaborating with other universities, companies, or organisations, ownership and IPR should be documented in a **consortium agreement** or **collaboration agreement**.
- Sometimes an additional **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)** governs confidential company data.
- Your **Faculty Contract Manager** can help draft and review these agreements:
  - https://intranet.tudelft.nl/en/-/faculty-contract-management?inheritRedirect=true

**Reuse of third-party data and software**
- Check and respect any data or software licences.
- Useful resources:
  - 4TU.ResearchData licensing: https://data.4tu.nl/info/en/use/publish-cite/upload-your-data-in-our-data-repository/licencing
  - The Turing Way – licensing: https://the-turing-way.netlify.app/reproducible-research/licensing
  - TU Delft Copyright Information Point: https://www.tudelft.nl/library/copyright

**Depositing data and code**
- TU Delft’s Research Data and Research Software policies expect you to deposit data, code and other materials needed to reproduce your findings in a suitable repository (e.g. 4TU.ResearchData), unless there are valid legal, ethical or contractual reasons not to.

In the DMP, explicitly mention which agreement governs IPR (e.g. consortium agreement, graduation agreement) and how this affects data sharing and reuse. If you tell me your role and partners, I can help you draft a specific paragraph.

context:
Please list the categories of data subjects and their geographical location.

Examples:
- Online focus groups: professionals in the domain of energy transition; all participants located in the EU (NL, DE, FR).
- In-person interviews: residents in urban areas in the USA, e.g. Cypress Park, Los Angeles.
- Categories: (healthy) adults, customers, consumers, (school) children, (hospital) patients, mentally or physically handicapped individuals, students, elderly, elected officials, prisoners, whistleblowers, product users, employees, entrepreneurs, app-users, felons, residents of a certain city, asylum seekers, specific professionals, general public in a certain area.

If unsure, contact the Privacy Team: privacy-tud@tudelft.nl.

Research abroad:
- Follow travel safety guidance (including Ministry of Foreign Affairs travel advice).
- Students travelling abroad for study, exchange, research or internships must follow the Travel Safety Protocol.

Links:
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
- Travel safety (intranet): https://intranet.tudelft.nl/-/reisveiligheid-2-1?p_l_back_url=%2Fsearch%3Fq%3Dtravel%2Bsafety&p_l_back_url_title=Search
- NL Ministry of Foreign Affairs travel advice: https://www.nederlandwereldwijd.nl/reizen-naar-het-buitenland
- TU Delft study abroad info: https://www.tudelft.nl/studenten/me-studentenportal/onderwijs/gerelateerd/study-abroad-me-new/outgoing
query: How do I describe the categories and geographical locations of my data subjects in the DMP?
answer:
In this DMP section you identify **who** your participants are and **where** they are located. This is important for ethics, privacy, and sometimes for data-transfer rules.

**Categories of data subjects**
- Describe participants in terms of group characteristics rather than individual identities, for example:
  - Healthy adults, TU Delft students, patients, employees, customers, app-users, residents of a particular city, etc.
- Indicate any vulnerable groups (e.g. children, elderly, mentally or physically handicapped individuals, asylum seekers), because these may require extra safeguards.

**Geographical location**
- Specify in which country or region participants are located, e.g.:
  - Professionals in the energy-transition sector located in EU countries (NL, DE, FR).
  - Residents of Cypress Park, Los Angeles, USA.

**Research abroad and travel safety**
- If you or your students collect data abroad, follow TU Delft’s travel safety guidance:
  - Intranet page on travel safety: https://intranet.tudelft.nl/-/reisveiligheid-2-1?p_l_back_url=%2Fsearch%3Fq%3Dtravel%2Bsafety&p_l_back_url_title=Search
  - Dutch Ministry of Foreign Affairs travel advice: https://www.nederlandwereldwijd.nl/reizen-naar-het-buitenland

If you are unsure how to classify your data subjects or have complex settings (e.g. multi-country, vulnerable groups), contact the **Privacy Team** at privacy-tud@tudelft.nl and mention in the DMP that you have done so. Tell me your study design and I can help you phrase a concise description.

context:
Will you be receiving personal data from or transferring personal data to third parties?

Guidance:
- Transfers of personally identifiable information between entities must be governed by agreements that clarify responsibilities towards data subjects.
- Indicate whether you will receive personal data (e.g. names, email addresses) from or send such data to third parties (companies, universities, NGOs, etc.).
- When sharing personal data with a third party, contractual arrangements (e.g. data processing agreements) are required.
- Check the privacy website for more information and contact the Privacy Team for support.

Links:
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
- Processing agreements: https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Verwerkersovereenkomst.aspx
query: How should I describe in my DMP whether I will receive or transfer personal data to third parties, and what agreements are required?
answer:
In this DMP section you should state clearly whether personal data will be **received from** or **sent to** third parties, and how the transfer is governed.

**Describe the flows of personal data**
- Indicate if you will:
  - Receive personal data from partners (e.g. a company providing customer data, another university providing a dataset).
  - Share personal data with partners (e.g. a subcontractor doing transcription, a collaborator doing analysis).

**Agreements and responsibilities**
- Explain which agreements will govern these transfers, for example:
  - Data processing agreements.
  - Collaboration or consortium agreements with specific data-protection clauses.
- These agreements should clarify roles (controller/processor), responsibilities, security measures, and retention.

TU Delft guidance (internal SharePoint):
- Data processing agreements: https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Verwerkersovereenkomst.aspx

**Privacy Team support**
- If you are unsure what type of agreement is needed, or you have not yet arranged it, contact the **Privacy Team** and mention this in the DMP:
  - privacy-tud@tudelft.nl

In your DMP, briefly describe the nature of the personal data being transferred, who the third parties are (by type) and which agreements will be used to manage the transfer.

context:
Which countries will you be receiving personal data from or transferring personal data to?

Guidance:
- TU Delft must maintain a record of transfers of personal data to recipients outside the EEA.
- Such transfers are generally prohibited unless:
  • the recipient is in a jurisdiction with an adequate level of data protection;
  • appropriate safeguards are in place; or
  • a derogation/exemption applies.
- Knowing from which countries personal data are received and to which countries they are transferred helps TU Delft implement appropriate measures.

Links:
- EEA countries overview: https://www.government.nl/topics/european-union/eu-eea-efta-and-schengen-area-countries
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
query: How do I document in the DMP which countries I receive personal data from or transfer personal data to, especially for transfers outside the EEA?
answer:
Here you should specify the **countries** involved in any personal data flows into or out of TU Delft.

**Within the EEA**
- List the EEA countries from which you receive personal data or to which you transfer them (e.g. NL, DE, FR). These countries share a common GDPR framework.
- EEA overview: https://www.government.nl/topics/european-union/eu-eea-efta-and-schengen-area-countries

**Outside the EEA**
- If data are transferred to or from countries **outside** the EEA, you must indicate this and explain the legal basis for the transfer, for example:
  - The country has an adequacy decision.
  - Standard contractual clauses or other appropriate safeguards are in place.
  - A specific derogation applies.

**Support from the Privacy Team**
- For complex data-transfer setups, or to confirm that appropriate safeguards are in place, contact the **Privacy Team** at privacy-tud@tudelft.nl and mention their advice in your DMP.

In your DMP, list the relevant countries and note whether each is in the EEA or not, and state that transfers outside the EEA will follow GDPR and TU Delft guidance.

context:
What advice did the Privacy team give regarding data transfer?

Guidance:
- If you selected options in Question 19 indicating high-risk processing, you must contact the Privacy Team for an assessment, which may include advice on data transfers and the need for a DPIA.
- You should report here the advice you received regarding data transfer agreements, safeguards, and conditions.

Links:
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
query: What should I write in the DMP under the question about the advice given by the Privacy Team regarding data transfer?
answer:
This section documents the **outcome of your consultation** with the Privacy Team about data transfers.

If you have already contacted the Privacy Team (e.g. because you process personal data in a potentially high-risk way or transfer data outside the EEA), summarise their advice, for example:

> I consulted the TU Delft Privacy Team on [date]. They advised that personal data transfers to [country/organisation] must be covered by [type of agreement, e.g. data processing agreement/standard contractual clauses], that only pseudonymised data will be shared, and that access will be limited to [roles]. A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) [is/is not] required.

If you **have not yet** contacted the Privacy Team but plan to, you can write, for example:

> I will consult the TU Delft Privacy Team (privacy-tud@tudelft.nl) regarding data transfers, and I will implement their recommendations, including any required agreements and safeguards.

Make sure the DMP is updated once you receive their final advice. For support, contact the Privacy Team at **privacy-tud@tudelft.nl**.

context:
What are the legal grounds for personal data processing?

Guidance:
- To process personal data you need a legal basis under the GDPR. At TU Delft, the preferred basis for research is **informed consent**, but other bases may apply depending on the context.
- If another legal basis is required, consult the Privacy Team.
- The Privacy Team provides guidance on GDPR principles (including legal basis).

Links:
- GDPR principles and legal basis (internal): https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/AVG-Principes.aspx#legal-basis
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
- Research Design 3 (communicating and managing risk): https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics/research-design-3-communicating-and-managing-risk
query: How do I choose and describe the legal grounds for personal data processing in my DMP at TU Delft?
answer:
Every processing of personal data in your project must have a valid **legal basis** under the GDPR. TU Delft often uses **informed consent** for research, but other legal bases may be applicable depending on the context and role of TU Delft.

**Common legal basis in research at TU Delft**
- **Informed consent** from participants, especially for typical academic research projects.

Other possible bases (less common, situation-dependent):
- Performance of a task carried out in the public interest.
- Legitimate interests (subject to balancing test), etc.

TU Delft internal guidance on legal bases (SharePoint):
- https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/AVG-Principes.aspx#legal-basis

**What to write in the DMP**
- State which legal basis you rely on and why it is appropriate, for example:

> The legal basis for processing personal data in this project is informed consent from participants. Participants will receive detailed information and will be asked to provide consent before any data collection.

- If consent is not appropriate or feasible, contact the **Privacy Team** at privacy-tud@tudelft.nl to identify the correct basis and mention this consultation in the DMP.

Additional ethical guidance (risk and communication):
- https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics/research-design-3-communicating-and-managing-risk

If you describe your study set-up, I can help you decide how to phrase the legal basis section.

context:
Please describe the informed consent procedure you will follow.

Guidance:
- The Human Research Ethics Committee (HREC) provides a guide and templates for informed consent materials.
- If you have questions, consult HREC documentation or your Faculty Data Steward.
- Example 1 (written informed consent): information sheet + signed physical/digital consent form before data collection.
- Example 2 (verbal informed consent): verbal consent recorded via audio/video or witnessed.
- Example 3 (anonymous online survey): opening statement explaining goals and procedures; consent implied by clicking through.

Links:
- Informed consent templates and guide: https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics/informed-consent-templates-and-guide
- Research Design 3: https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics/research-design-3-communicating-and-managing-risk
- HREC main page: https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics
- Data Stewardship contact: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/
query: How should I describe my informed consent procedure in the DMP for a TU Delft project involving human participants?
answer:
In this section you should explain **how** you will inform participants and obtain their consent for study participation and data processing.

**Key components**
- What information participants receive (goals, procedures, risks/benefits, data types, retention, sharing).
- How they give consent (written, verbal, or implied for anonymous online surveys).
- How consent is documented and stored.

**Example formulations**
- **Written consent**:
> Potential participants will receive an information sheet describing the study purpose, procedures and data processing. They will sign a [physical/digital] informed consent form before participation.

- **Verbal consent**:
> Participants will be informed verbally about the study and data processing. Their consent will be recorded via [audio/video recording or written note with a witness].

- **Anonymous online survey**:
> At the start of the survey, an opening statement will explain the study and data processing. Proceeding to the survey indicates participants’ agreement with these terms.

**Support and templates**
- TU Delft informed-consent templates and guide: https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics/informed-consent-templates-and-guide
- Additional guidance (Research Design 3): https://www.tudelft.nl/over-tu-delft/strategie/integriteitsbeleid/human-research-ethics/research-design-3-communicating-and-managing-risk
- You can also contact your **Faculty Data Steward** for help: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/

Tell me what kind of study you do (interviews, experiments, surveys) and I can help you pick and adapt one of these patterns.

context:
Where will you store the physical/digital signed consent forms or other types of proof of consent?

Guidance:
- Hard copy consent forms: store in a secure location (locked cabinet/office).
- Digital consent forms: store securely and separately from the research data (e.g. encrypted folder) to avoid re-identification.
- Signed consent forms must be retained even if research data are anonymised and no longer considered personal data.
- For help, contact your Faculty Data Steward.

Links:
- Data Stewardship contact: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/
query: How should I describe where and how I will store signed informed consent forms or other proof of consent in my DMP?
answer:
TU Delft expects signed consent forms or other proof of consent to be stored securely and separately from the main research dataset.

**Physical consent forms**
- Store paper forms in a locked cabinet or locked office space with limited access.
- Mention in the DMP that physical forms will be kept in secure storage at your faculty/department.

**Digital consent forms**
- Store digital copies in a secure location, separate from the research data, preferably in an encrypted folder or drive.
- Ensure that any key linking participant identifiers to data is stored separately from both the data and the consent forms.

**Retention**
- Note that even if you later anonymise the data, you must still keep the consent forms for validation and audit purposes, in line with TU Delft and funder requirements.

In the DMP, briefly describe the secure storage location and separation from the research data. For project-specific advice, you can contact your **Faculty Data Steward**: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/.

context:
Does the processing of the personal data result in a high risk to the data subjects?

Guidance:
- If processing could result in high risk to participants, you must contact the Privacy Team to assess whether a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** is needed.
- High-risk situations may include:
  - Evaluation or scoring of people (profiling, predicting performance, economic situation, health, preferences, behaviour, location or movements).
  - Automated decision-making with legal or similarly significant effects.
  - Systematic monitoring of individuals (e.g. tracking movements on campus or in cities).
  - Processing of sensitive personal data (e.g. financial data, location data, data on vulnerable groups; photos and videos of participants).
  - Processing of special categories of personal data (religion, race, health, sexual life, political preference, trade union membership, genetic/biometric data, criminal offences, BSN).
  - Large-scale processing, combining datasets, vulnerable data subjects, innovative technologies, or processing that may prevent data subjects from exercising rights or using services.

Links:
- TU Delft guidance on sensitive personal data (PDF): https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SiteAssets/Forms/AllItems.aspx?id=%2Fsites%2FSecurityPrivacyTUD%2FSiteAssets%2FSitePages%2FAVG%2DBegrippenlijst%2FTU%2DDelft%2DGuidance%2Dsensitive%2Dpersonal%2Ddata%5Fversion%2DFebruary%2D2023%2D%2Deng%2D%2Epdf
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
- Data Stewardship contact: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/
query: How do I determine whether my personal data processing is high risk and what should I state in the DMP if it is?
answer:
You should assess whether your project involves **high-risk processing** of personal data. If it might, TU Delft requires you to contact the Privacy Team to determine if a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is needed.

**Indicators of high risk**
Your processing may be high-risk if it includes, for example:
- Profiling, evaluation or scoring of individuals (e.g. performance, behaviour, credit-worthiness).
- Automated decision-making with legal or similarly significant effects.
- Systematic monitoring of people (e.g. tracking movements, monitoring online behaviour).
- Handling sensitive personal data (e.g. financial data, detailed location data, vulnerable groups, photos/videos of participants).
- Processing of special categories of data (e.g. race, religion, health, sexual life, political opinions, union membership, genetic or biometric data, criminal offences, BSN).
- Large-scale or long-term processing, combining datasets, or use of innovative technologies.

TU Delft guidance on sensitive personal data:
- https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SiteAssets/Forms/AllItems.aspx?id=%2Fsites%2FSecurityPrivacyTUD%2FSiteAssets%2FSitePages%2FAVG%2DBegrippenlijst%2FTU%2DDelft%2DGuidance%2Dsensitive%2Dpersonal%2Ddata%5Fversion%2DFebruary%2D2023%2D%2Deng%2D%2Epdf

**What to state in the DMP**
- If you suspect high risk, write that you will consult the **Privacy Team** (privacy-tud@tudelft.nl) about the need for a DPIA.
- Once you receive their assessment, update the DMP to indicate whether a DPIA is required and summarise any key recommendations.

For help with the assessment, you can also talk to your **Faculty Data Steward**: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/library/current-topics/research-data-management/r/support/data-stewardship/contact/.

If you describe your planned processing, I can help you identify potential risk indicators.

context:
Did the Privacy Team advise you to perform a DPIA?

Answer options:
- Yes – please provide details in the additional information box below.
- No – please provide details in the additional information box below.
- Privacy Team has not yet been contacted / Privacy Team has been contacted, awaiting response.

If you selected options in Question 19 indicating high risk, you must contact the Privacy Team (privacy-tud@tudelft.nl) and then report their assessment here.

Links:
- Privacy Team: mailto:privacy-tud@tudelft.nl
query: What should I write in the DMP about whether the Privacy Team advised me to perform a DPIA?
answer:
This question records the **outcome of discussions with the Privacy Team** about the need for a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA).

Depending on your situation, you can write:

1. **DPIA required**
> I consulted the TU Delft Privacy Team (privacy-tud@tudelft.nl) on [date]. They advised that a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is required due to [brief reason, e.g. large-scale processing of sensitive personal data]. A DPIA will be carried out and its recommendations implemented.

2. **DPIA not required**
> I consulted the TU Delft Privacy Team on [date]. They advised that a DPIA is not required for this project, provided that the described safeguards (pseudonymisation, restricted access, secure storage, etc.) are implemented.

3. **Consultation pending**
> I will consult the TU Delft Privacy Team (privacy-tud@tudelft.nl) regarding the need for a DPIA. The DMP will be updated once their advice is received.

Make sure your answer is consistent with Question 19 and with any actual emails or documents from the Privacy Team.

context:
How much data/code storage will you require during the project lifetime?

Options:
- < 250 GB
- 250 GB – 5 TB
- > 5 TB

Guidance:
- The PI can request a dedicated Project Data Storage drive via the self-service portal (TopDesk). Requests > 5 TB must be approved by the Faculty IT Manager.
- This section is followed by an overview of data classification and storage containers/services.

Links:
- Project Data Storage request: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- Data classification policy (internal): https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Beleid-Dataclassificatie.aspx
query: How should I estimate and report how much data and code storage I will need during my project at TU Delft?
answer:
In this DMP section you provide a rough estimate of the storage capacity your project requires so that appropriate infrastructure (e.g. Project Data Storage) can be provisioned.

**Estimating storage needs**
- Consider expected size of:
  - Raw and processed datasets.
  - Intermediate analysis files.
  - Code, documentation and possibly large outputs (e.g. model checkpoints, simulation results).
- Choose one of the categories:
  - **< 250 GB**
  - **250 GB – 5 TB**
  - **> 5 TB**

**Requesting Project Data Storage**
- The PI or project owner can request a **Project Data Storage** (U: drive) via TopDesk:
  - https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- Requests above 5 TB generally require approval by the Faculty IT Manager.

**Data classification**
- When estimating space, also consider the data classification (Confidential, Highly Confidential, etc.), as this affects where data may be stored:
  - Internal policy: https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Beleid-Dataclassificatie.aspx

In your DMP, indicate the chosen storage band and explain that data will be stored on Project Data Storage, with additional services (e.g. GitLab, 4TU.ResearchData) as needed. If you describe your data types, I can help you sanity-check your estimate.

context:
Overview of data classification and storage containers/services.

The document lists several storage options with brief descriptions and intended data classifications. External services like Dropbox and personal Google Drive are discouraged.

Examples of containers:
1. Exchange Online (email): mailbox, calendar; not intended as storage/archiving; mainly for data up to Confidential; for Highly Confidential data use additional measures (e.g. SURFfilesender, Zivver).
2. OneDrive for Business: up to 300 GB for personal documents; not recommended for research data; data deleted when leaving TU Delft.
3. Individual home drive (H:): 8 GB; not suitable for research data; deleted when leaving TU Delft.
4. SURFdrive: up to 500 GB; temporary personal storage; not recommended for research data; limited backup.
5. Staff Group Data (M:): shared departmental storage for business documents; not recommended as primary research data storage.
6. Staff Bulk Data (N:): temporary large-volume storage; no backup; being phased out; not recommended for research data.
7. Project Data (U:): primary storage for project/research data; owner controls access; suitable up to Confidential (extra measures for Highly Confidential).
8. SharePoint: publishing business documents; some collaboration features.
9. SURFfilesender: large file sharing (up to ~1 TB); suitable up to Highly Confidential with encryption.
10. 4TU.ResearchData: long-term archive and repository for research data; mainly public data and embargoed datasets.
11. FTP account: temporary data exchange; no backup; not for structural storage.

Links:
- Project Data Storage: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- Exchange Online service: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=8754f6247c93407db5e648a12016f915
- OneDrive manuals: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/it-manuals/onedrive
- SURFdrive: https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surfdrive
- SURFfilesender: https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surffilesender
- Data classification policy: https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Beleid-Dataclassificatie.aspx
- 4TU.ResearchData: https://data.4tu.nl/info/
query: What is the overview of TU Delft data storage containers and which services should I use for research data and sharing?
answer:
TU Delft offers several storage and sharing services, each intended for specific use cases and data classifications. In your DMP you should show that you choose containers that match your data type and sensitivity.

**Recommended for research data**
- **Project Data (U: drive)** – primary storage for project and research data (up to Confidential; add encryption for Highly Confidential). Access is controlled by the project owner. Request via TopDesk:
  - https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- **4TU.ResearchData** – trusted repository for publishing and archiving research data, usually as open or embargoed datasets:
  - https://data.4tu.nl/info/

**Not recommended as primary research storage**
- **OneDrive for Business**, **H: drive**, **SURFdrive** – mainly for personal documents; access may be lost when leaving TU Delft; limited backup.
- **Staff Group Data (M:)** and **Staff Bulk Data (N:)** – for departmental business documents or temporary bulk storage; not ideal for research data, especially not for confidential data.

**Email and file transfer**
- **Exchange Online (email)** – not intended as a storage/archiving solution; avoid sending large amounts of personal data by email.
- **SURFfilesender** – for large file transfers (up to ~1 TB). For Highly Confidential data, encrypt files and send passwords via a separate channel:
  - https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surffilesender

**Collaboration and publishing**
- **SharePoint/Teams** – for collaboration and publishing business or project documents.
- **GitLab (TU Delft instance)** and **GitHub** – for version control of code; choose TU Delft GitLab when code is confidential, and external Git hosting for open collaboration.

**Data classification**
- Always consider the data classification (e.g. Confidential, Highly Confidential) when choosing containers:
  - https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Beleid-Dataclassificatie.aspx

In your DMP, you can state that primary research data will be stored on Project Data (U: drive), code in GitLab/GitHub as appropriate, and final datasets in 4TU.ResearchData, with temporary sharing via SURFfilesender if needed.

context:
7. Where will the data/code be stored and backed-up during the project lifetime?

Guidance:
- **Project Data Storage (U: drive)** is the TU Delft storage facility with backup for research projects; only project members have access. Recommended for all research data, especially personal/confidential data. Additional encryption is advised for highly confidential data (see \"Sharing and collaborating\" SharePoint page).
- External collaborators can be given access to the entire Project Data Storage drive.
- Project Data Storage can be requested via TopDesk; changes (size, access) via faculty Service Desk or UMRA.
- **OneDrive** and **SURFdrive**: up to 1 TB; only temporary storage; access ends when leaving TU Delft; not for highly confidential data.
- **SURFfilesender**: large file sharing (up to 1 TB); encrypt highly confidential data and send passwords via a separate channel.
- **GitLab (TU Delft)** and **Subversion**: version control systems with backup for code; external access possible via request.
- **GitHub and other external git platforms**: suitable when access restrictions are not required.
- Local drives, external hard drives, and Staff Group Data (M:) should not be the primary storage for research data, especially not confidential data.

Links:
- Project Data Storage (TopDesk): https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
- Sharing and collaborating (SharePoint): https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Gegevens-delen-en-samenwerken.aspx
- Service desks: https://intranet.tudelft.nl/-/service-desks
- UMRA manual: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/it-manuals/umra
- OneDrive manual: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/it-manuals/onedrive
- SURFdrive: https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surfdrive
- SURFfilesender: https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surffilesender
- GitLab TU Delft: https://gitlab.tudelft.nl/
- Subversion service: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=9db38a0434fc4453a9bfcde1436d18aa
- GitHub: https://github.com/
query: Where should I state that my data and code will be stored and backed up during the project lifetime in the DMP, and which TU Delft services should I mention?
answer:
In your DMP you should demonstrate that data and code will be stored on reliable infrastructure with appropriate backup, access control and, when needed, encryption.

**Data storage and backup**
- Use **Project Data Storage (U: drive)** as your primary location for research data:
  - It provides regular backup and controlled access for project members.
  - Request via TopDesk: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=846ebb16181c43b5836c063a917dd199
  - For highly confidential data, apply additional encryption and follow TU Delft \"Sharing and collaborating\" guidance: https://tud365.sharepoint.com/sites/SecurityPrivacyTUD/SitePages/en/Gegevens-delen-en-samenwerken.aspx

**Cloud and temporary storage**
- **OneDrive** and **SURFdrive** can be used for temporary storage and sharing (up to ~1 TB), but not for highly confidential data and not as the only location. Access ends when you leave TU Delft.
  - OneDrive: https://www.tudelft.nl/en/it-manuals/onedrive
  - SURFdrive: https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surfdrive
- **SURFfilesender** is suitable for large file transfers; encrypt highly confidential data and send passwords separately:
  - https://www.surf.nl/en/services/surffilesender

**Code repositories**
- Use **TU Delft GitLab** or **Subversion** for version-controlled storage of research code with backup:
  - GitLab: https://gitlab.tudelft.nl/
  - Subversion service: https://tudelft.topdesk.net/tas/public/ssp/content/detail/service?unid=9db38a0434fc4453a9bfcde1436d18aa
- Use GitHub or other external platforms if access restrictions are not needed: https://github.com/

**Avoid** using local drives, external USB disks or Staff Group Data (M:) as primary storage, especially for confidential data.

In your DMP, state that data will be stored on Project Data Storage with regular backups, that appropriate repository services will be used for code, and that any cloud or sharing tools follow TU Delft Security & Privacy guidance.

context:
What documentation will accompany data/code?

Guidance:
- Documentation should be sufficient to validate, reproduce, or reuse data/code.
- Start documenting early in the project.
- Important elements:
  - Methodology of data collection (enabling replication; may be described in protocols, including protocols.io).
  - Codebooks and data dictionaries (e.g. for survey/interview data).
  - README files describing datasets and how to use them (4TU.ResearchData provides README guidelines).
  - Protocols and data processing procedures (especially for complex workflows).
  - Metadata (embedded metadata, additional documentation, discovery metadata, disciplinary standards).
  - Software documentation (README, usage docs, developer/API docs, inline comments and docstrings).

Links:
- Codebook guides: https://atlasti.com/research-hub/codebook-qualitative-research, https://ddialliance.org/training/getting-started-new-content/create-a-codebook, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/ICPSR/cms/1983
- Data dictionary guide (OSF): https://help.osf.io/article/217-how-to-make-a-data-dictionary
- 4TU.ResearchData portal: https://data.4tu.nl/portal
- 4TU README guidelines: https://data.4tu.nl/s/documents/Guidelines_for_creating_a_README_file.pdf
- protocols.io: https://www.protocols.io/
- DataCite metadata: https://schema.datacite.org/
- Dublin Core: https://dublincore.org/
- FAIRsharing (disciplinary standards): https://fairsharing.org/standards/
- Numpydoc docstring format: https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html
- AutoDocstring VS Code extension: https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=njpwerner.autodocstring
- FAIR code example: https://github.com/manuGil/fair-code/tree/main
- ReadTheDocs: https://docs.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
- GitHub wikis: https://docs.github.com/en/communities/documenting-your-project-with-wikis/about-wikis
- TU Delft DCC FAIR software checklist: https://tu-delft-dcc.github.io/docs/software/checklist.html
query: What kinds of documentation should accompany my research data and code according to TU Delft RDM guidelines, and how can I describe this in the DMP?
answer:
TU Delft expects you to provide enough documentation for others (and yourself in the future) to **understand, validate and reuse** your data and code. In the DMP you should summarise what types of documentation you will create.

**For data**
- **Methodology description** – how data were collected/produced, including instruments, protocols, and processing steps. You can formalise protocols using services like https://www.protocols.io/.
- **Codebooks / data dictionaries** – especially for survey and interview data, explaining variables, codes, and categories:
  - Codebook guides: https://atlasti.com/research-hub/codebook-qualitative-research, https://ddialliance.org/training/getting-started-new-content/create-a-codebook, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/ICPSR/cms/1983
  - Data dictionary guide: https://help.osf.io/article/217-how-to-make-a-data-dictionary
- **README files** – for each dataset or package, describing content, structure, formats, units, and usage. 4TU.ResearchData provides templates:
  - https://data.4tu.nl/s/documents/Guidelines_for_creating_a_README_file.pdf
- **Metadata** – descriptive information about creators, time, place, methods, and file formats. Data repositories like 4TU.ResearchData use standards such as **DataCite** and **Dublin Core**:
  - DataCite: https://schema.datacite.org/
  - Dublin Core: https://dublincore.org/
  - FAIRsharing (disciplinary standards): https://fairsharing.org/standards/

**For software/code**
- **Usage documentation** – a README describing purpose, installation, required dependencies, and example usage. See examples in the FAIR code repository: https://github.com/manuGil/fair-code/tree/main
- **Developer/API documentation** – for larger projects, additional docs (Markdown, PDF, ReadTheDocs, GitHub wikis):
  - ReadTheDocs: https://docs.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
  - GitHub wikis: https://docs.github.com/en/communities/documenting-your-project-with-wikis/about-wikis
- **Inline documentation** – comments and docstrings in the code (e.g. using the Numpydoc format and tools like AutoDocstring in VS Code):
  - Numpydoc: https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html
  - AutoDocstring: https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=njpwerner.autodocstring

In your DMP, list which documentation types you will provide (e.g. README + codebook + metadata + software README/API docs) and state that they will be stored together with the data/code and included when depositing in a repository such as 4TU.ResearchData: https://data.4tu.nl/portal.

If you tell me your discipline and typical outputs, I can suggest a minimal documentation bundle tailored to your project.
